data.title: GE HealthCare: A Better Proposition Here
data.description:
Shares of GE HealthCare (GEHC) have failed to choose direction in recent times, as a sluggish share price makes it so that appeal has improved a bit.

Back in November of last year, I urged a word of caution after GE HealthCare announced a substantial acquisition, as operating margins were impacted substantially by tariffs. At the time, shares had enjoyed solid momentum so far that year, pushing up valuation multiples along the way.

Shares have been trending stagnant over the past half a year, while modest growth is delivered upon. This is a combination that improves appeal a bit, with growth being relatively solid, set to continue into 2026, making now quite an opportune time to get involved with the shares.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

About the Results

In February, GE HealthCare reported a near 5% increase in full-year sales to $20.6 billion, with organic growth rates reported at 3.5%. Momentum was more pronounced in the final quarter, when reported sales topped 7% as organic growth rates approached 5%.

The business continues to rely on its core imaging revenue business, which generates over $9 billion in sales, responsible for nearly half of total revenues. Despite its large sales base and the importance of scanning and imaging devices supplied to hospitals, it is actually among the lower-margin activities in the portfolio.

Advanced visualization is a +$5 billion business, with segment margins in the twenties, as its 2D/3D visualization tools are essential, for instance, in surgery. Patient care generates just over $3 billion in sales, these revenues actually falling slightly from the year before, including ESG solutions in diagnostics and treatment plans. Pharmaceutical diagnostics is a near $3 billion business, a highly profitable one, and a rapidly growing segment, driven by organic and inorganic activities.

The modest growth on the topline translated nicely onto the bottom line, with operating profits up in the mid-single digits as well. EBIT was reported at $2.76 billion, as GAAP earnings came in at $2.08 billion, equal to $4.55 per share, that number up twenty-one cents on the year before. Following a string of adjustments, both negative and positive, adjusted earnings of $4.59 per share were largely in line with GAAP numbers, although these earnings were only up by ten cents.

This looks like a relatively modest advancement but comes with about a half a dollar per share headwind from tariffs, as otherwise the performance has been strong, offset by internal cost-saving measures and compensation for tariff costs.

The 2026 guidance was solid, with ongoing growth rates seen between 3-4%. This was accompanied by an anticipated 50-80 basis point improvement in adjusted EBIT margins, which is seen driving earnings up to $4.95-$5.15 per share, up 8-12% on the year before. This margin expansion is anticipated thanks to lower tariff costs.

The company ended the year with $5.5 billion in net debt, with adjusted EBIT for the year reported at $3.2 billion, as I peg realistic adjusted EBITDA around $3.5 billion. This is no issue, but pending M&A action closing, the debt load is seen inching up.

Finding Its Footing

Since its separation from GE in 2023, GE HealthCare has created a world in which healthcare has no limits, the company being a powerhouse in its field. The company has seen modest sales growth since its separation, accompanied by modest margin gains, as the company has made modest inroads from a performance perspective.

This came as the company has been fighting off some headwinds, including tough conditions in China and the impact of tariffs, among others. However, most of these headwinds appear to be a thing of the past, as the company has been making some inroads.

Trading at $75 in November, GE HealthCare commanded a $34 billion equity valuation based on a share tally of 457 million shares, as a net debt load of $6 billion translated into a $40 billion enterprise valuation. That was equivalent to 2 times sales, 12 times EBITDA, with equity trading at 16-17 times adjusted earnings.

That was all about to change a little bit as the company announced a $2.3 billion deal to acquire medical imaging software provider Intelerad. Valued at 8.5 times sales of $270 million, the acquisition multiple was steep, although those 30% EBITDA margins are pretty compelling, implying that the business was valued at 28 times EBITDA.

Based on pro forma net debt of $9 billion and $3.4 billion in EBITDA, I saw leverage ratios in the mid-2s, as earnings multiples were becoming more demanding but were far from demanding at the same time.

Coming in a Bit

Since November, shares have lost about $2, trading at $73 here. This implies that the market value has declined by a billion, for a >$33 billion equity valuation, while net debt came down to $5.5 billion ahead of the Intelerad deal closing, which happened in March.

This is seen boosting net debt to $7.8 billion, as leverage is quickly coming down from mid-2s, or even a bit higher, to 2.3 times here. Note that GE HealthCare sees low-double-digit growth rates for the business with slight dilution seen in earnings per share in year one; this, however, is not quantified and, moreover, is seen being largely compensated by cost-cutting efforts.

That is encouraging, and based on the higher earnings outlook for 2025, earnings multiples have come in at 14-15 times. This combination of slightly lower leverage, lower share price, higher earnings, and overall lower valuations makes that appeal on the increase.

What Now?

With a quarterly dividend of a mere three and a half cents per share, the 0.2% dividend yield is very modest, but it allows for a rapid reduction in the leverage of GE HealthCare here. This is encouraging, as the company sees solid growth in 2025. A solid backlog backs up the guidance. This stood at $21.8 billion by year-end, exceeding reported sales, as this backlog rose by some two billion during the year.

Overall, the picture and performance are quite solid, which, combined with a slightly lagging share price, makes appeal on the increase in my view. This certainly is the case, as the company has mid-term ambitions to boost adjusted EBITDA margins from high teens to 20% of sales. That would imply a couple of points increase, as every point increase could boost earnings by over thirty cents per share, creating a credible roadmap for earnings to advance to $6 per share in the coming years.

After I was cautious in the fall, my regard for GE HealthCare and the risk-reward proposition is increasing here, as I am contemplating a position here at current levels, with shares appearing to be a bit undercovered here.

data.title: Guidewire Software: Growth Story Is Even More Durable Today
data.description:
Investment Action

I continue to rate Guidewire Software (GWRE) a buy because the business has shown broader demand and stronger durability than before. Demand is no longer supported by one large customer win alone. Instead, GWRE is seeing broader cloud adoption, deeper customer commitments, and early signs that AI is helping drive more demand around the core platform.

We Are Past the Inflection Point

I was bullish on GWRE in my previous write-up as cloud adoption was accelerating and GWRE was seeing record deal momentum. The concern was whether GWRE can continue that momentum from the inflection point and not just be driven by one very large Liberty Mutual win. I think that concern has eased. Management said GWRE closed another 15 InsuranceSuite cloud deals and 2 InsuranceNow deals in Q2, and deal activity in Q2 also included 3 new customer wins along with healthy migrations and expansions. Some examples include

  • Aviva UK committed to move all of its Guidewire estate to the GWRE platform;
  • Tokio Marine North America is preparing to migrate major elements of three US carrier businesses;
  • Donegal Insurance Group selected GWRE Cloud as the next step in its core system modernization strategy; and
  • One of Canada's largest private insurers also signed on to modernize its legacy claims administration system to ClaimCenter.

As you can see, GWRE is no longer relying on one major customer to support the equity story. It is winning different types of work across different regions and from customers at different stages of cloud adoption. That signals that the demand is broad-based. It was also noted that two customers will see their ARR grow to >$20 million during the committed period. That also gives a good sense that GWRE is able to really win a sizeable wallet share.

The Business Now Looks More Durable

In my view, the management gave much stronger proof that the business is durable. The main data point to track here is still fully ramped Annual Recurring Revenue [ARR], which reached $1.42 billion in Q2 and is still growing faster than reported ARR (reported ARR was $1.121 billion, up 22% y/y). As I said before, so long as fully ramped ARR is pacing ahead of reported ARR, it means that GWRE continues to sign contract value that has not yet fully shown in the reported ARR base. In other words, the growth runway is still larger than the headline number suggests.

The other important data points that support my view are seen in contract length and backlog. Management said the average contract duration of new InsuranceSuite cloud deals over the last 12 months was >6 years, and that is a positive surprise since the usual cloud arrangement is 5 years. Add on the fact that remaining performance obligations [RPO] reached $3.5 billion, up 63% y/y; all of this suggests that the customers are signing larger and longer commitments. Logically, a customer does not sign a longer contract for a system if they don't see the value. Hence, I take this as a strong signal that GWRE is becoming more important to customers' workflow.

All the above points to one thing, that is, the business looks more durable now.

AI

Previously, my view on AI was that it is mostly an extra benefit that could "strengthen" the GWRE platform over time. I have already touched on insurers need to adopt GWRE's modern platform to fully adopt AI, so I won't repeat everything again. But as a quick recap, the idea is that older insurance systems were not built for real-time data, automation, or AI-driven workflows. They were built to process transactions, not to support intelligence across underwriting and claims. If insurers want to use AI properly, they first need a better core system and cleaner data. That is where GWRE's platform comes into play.

My view is still the same, but what has changed now is that management is directly linking generative AI to faster modernization demand. Put simply, AI is no longer just a feature; it seems to increasingly become the reason that pushes insurers to migrate. There are already early signs of this showing up in the business. For instance, GWRE closed 25 deals in Q2 that included one or more data and analytics products, and it also signed its first PricingCenter deal. ProNavigator is another example. Basically, this is an AI assistant built into GWRE's core system, and management said it got off to a strong start with nine deals in Q2. We know to a good extent this has a strong value proposition since customers such as Aviva Canada and Gore Mutual plan to use it inside the same system where their teams already work.

Valuation

I believe the stock is cheap at this valuation (7x forward revenue). Despite all the worries about AI entirely displacing GWRE (like using Claude to write the entire software through vibes), GWRE still grew 23% in FY2025, and management guided for 18% growth in FY2026 (in line with my previous model). If we extrapolate that 18% growth forward, GWRE should generate ~$2 billion in revenue by FY2028. Now, based on historicals, at 18% y/y growth, the stock should trade at least >9x forward revenue (an average of 9.2x is based on ~13% y/y growth). But say that even if valuation just stays at 7x, the stock is still worth 40% more than where it is.

Risk

GWRE is winning larger cloud projects, but these deals are still complex and can take longer if customers face internal delays or budget changes. That could slow ARR growth and revenue conversion even if demand stays strong. I would also watch how the AI story develops. If adoption is slower than expected, the growth upside from selling more around the platform may be smaller than what I expect.

Conclusion

I keep my buy rating on GWRE. In my view, the recent developments gave stronger proof that GWRE's growth is not a one-off. The business now looks broader, more durable, and harder to displace. Cloud demand is expanding across different insurers and regions, customer commitments are getting larger and longer, and AI is starting to support the core platform more directly.

data.title: John Hancock Diversified Macro Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • The fund incurred losses in Q4 2025 from positions in fixed income, equities, and commodities.
  • Risk assets remained resilient in Q4, although momentum waned as investors balanced supportive policy expectations against a growing list of risks, including fiscal sustainability, inflation pressures, slowing growth, and softer labor markets.
  • Trade policy volatility, elevated deficits, and geopolitical fragmentation have become embedded features of the macro landscape, influencing term premia, cross-asset correlations, and the reliability of historical diversification relationships.

Market review and outlook

The risk-on environment seen in the third quarter extended into Q4, despite continued uncertainty around trade policy, fiscal expansion, and geopolitical developments. October featured leadership changes in France and Japan, continued trade tensions between the United States and China, and an uncertain economic outlook that was further obscured by a lack of data due to the U.S. government shutdown. Equities extended their upward trajectory, supported by strong earnings, expectations for further monetary easing, and sustained enthusiasm around technology and AI. Global fixed income rallied meaningfully as markets absorbed the U.S. Federal Reserve's (Fed's) 25 basis-point rate cut and the prospect of further central bank easing across developed economies.

November began with uncertainty surrounding the U.S. government shutdown, while hawkish Fed commentary created doubts around a December rate cut, weighing on equity and fixed-income markets. When data ultimately arrived mid-month, it pointed to cooling consumption, softer labor-market momentum, and a notable deterioration in consumer sentiment, which renewed rate-cut expectations and led to a rally in global equity and fixed-income markets.

December saw markets finish the year on a cautiously optimistic note. U.S. equity performance was mixed as investors weighed another Fed rate cut and concerns about the sustainability of the AI rally. Non-U.S. equity markets generally outperformed, reflecting more attractive valuations and improved earnings. Front-end rates in the U.S. and Europe remained anchored by stable policy expectations, while long-end yields in the United States and Japan cheapened materially as growth resilience, supply, and normalization risks pushed term premia higher.

Looking ahead, markets in 2026 are likely to remain volatile and path-dependent. Rather than a single dominant macro narrative, investors may face a series of regime shifts driven by the sequencing of policy decisions, data releases, and geopolitical events. In our view, this backdrop favors flexibility, disciplined risk management, and an ability to adapt quickly as conditions evolve. While uncertainty remains elevated, this environment of dispersion and transition has historically created meaningful opportunities for macro-oriented strategies.

Contributors and detractors

Fixed-income positions generated losses in Q4, mainly from long positions in European, Japanese, Canadian, and Australian rates, as well as mixed positions along the U.S. yield curve. The portfolio incurred losses in equities from mixed positions in U.S. and European benchmark indexes, a short position in the FTSE 100 Index, and a long position in the Hang Seng Index. In commodities, mixed positions in energy markets led to modest losses, which were partially offset by gains from long positions in precious metals and copper. The portfolio recorded profits in currencies from a short position in the Japanese yen versus the U.S. dollar, with smaller gains from long positions in the Mexican peso and the euro versus the U.S. dollar.

Portfolio changes

Effective January 2026, we've included additional active, cross-sectional (multi-market) alpha signals in the fund. Whereas the fund previously applied directional signals independently across each of the 55 traded markets, the new alpha signals introduce multi-market "basket" trades that capture cross-market relationships. These additions seek to enhance diversification by broadening the opportunity set to include dispersion, cross-sectional relationships, curve-shape opportunities, and other multi-market dynamics. With this expansion of alpha signals in the fund, we also added 20 rates, equities, and currency markets, bringing the trading universe up from 55 markets to 75 markets. These markets have increased liquidity in recent years and can provide enhanced diversification benefits. Taken together, the expansion of markets and alpha signals is designed to broaden the opportunity set across varied market environments and improve the consistency of outcomes over time.

data.title: Cirrus Aircraft: The Only Pure-Play General Aviation Stock Is A Strong Buy
data.description:
The aerospace and defense industry is a big industry with many companies. However, there are few pure-play companies focused on general aviation. With this report, I am adding the only pure-play company that I could find, which is Cirrus Aircraft (CRRSF), and explaining why I believe the company is a buy.

Cirrus Aircraft Provides Premium Airplanes

Cirrus Aircraft provides premium airplanes, operating a business focused on aircraft owners and high-net-worth individuals. The company has two revenue buckets, which are aircraft manufacturing and services. Aircraft manufacturing is Cirrus’ core business, accounting for 85% of revenues with two main product lines. The first one is the SRX2 Series piston aircraft, and the second one is the Vision Jet. The company differentiates itself with safety systems such as the CAPS parachute and Safe Return Autoland feature.

The second revenue stream is the high-margin aftermarket business providing recurring revenues from maintenance, parts, upgrades, pilot training, aircraft management, digital services, and financing.

Double-Digit Growth on Revenues With a Slight Lift in Margins

In 2025, revenues increased 13.1% to $1.35 billion, with aircraft revenues growing 12.5% to $1.15 billion, driven by a combination of higher volume and mix, while services and other revenues increased 17% to $203 million. Gross profit grew 15.4% to $477.2 million, indicating an 80 basis point lift to the margins to 34.5% despite margin pressure from the G7+ launch. Typically, new aircraft come with lower initial margins, and that was no different for Cirrus, but even with that pressure absorbed, the margins still grew. Operating profit increased 13.1% to $176 million, indicating a margin expansion of 20 basis points to 13% despite higher marketing costs in connection with the G7+ launch. EBITDA grew 13.8% to $213 million, indicating a 10-basis-point margin expansion to 15.7%.

The Growth Drivers and Risks for Cirrus Aircraft

For Cirrus Aircraft, the growth driver is mainly the increase in production, which drives services revenues over the long term and a higher value mix. In 2025, the company increased deliveries to 797 units, and with a backlog of over 1,000 aircraft and continued order momentum, the company has strong multi-year visibility on revenue generation.

Beyond volume growth, product mix provides an additional tailwind. The Vision Jet is priced significantly above the SR2X platform and continues to support revenue growth and margin expansion as production scales further.

In terms of margins, there is not a lot of upside apart from G7+ margins maturing, mix shift, and the services growth boosting the company’s composite margins, but given that aircraft deliveries account for 85% of the revenues currently, the growth in services revenues will only provide incremental upside. Risks include any deterioration in demand, since this remains a cyclical market, as well as higher supply chain costs, as inflation and supply chain bottlenecks have significantly increased costs throughout the industries over the past years.

Cirrus Aircraft Initiated With a Strong Buy Rating

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Cirrus Aircraft trades under the OTC ticker CRRSF. We do note that at times liquidity may be lacking, giving investors a hard time buying or selling at desired quantities and volumes. Cirrus Aircraft’s ordinary shares trade at the Hong Kong Stock Exchange under the ticker 2507, which provides better liquidity.

I believe that Cirrus Aircraft is a strong buy with a $6.57 price target indicating 25% upside. This upside is supported by 13.6% annual sales growth, with EBITDA and free cash flow growing along the same lines. The company’s backlog and positioning as a premium airplane provider in general aviation, along with aftermarket sales opportunities, should provide long-term growth opportunities.

The margins are expected to dip this year, likely driven by the ramp-up of the G7+ program, but after that I do expect the company to see modest margin expansion along with strong free cash flow improvements with solid cash conversion. We also note that the company is in a net cash position, which, combined with positive free cash flows, should allow the company to continue investing in the business and grow shareholder returns.

Conclusion: Cirrus Aircraft Looks Attractive

I believe that Cirrus Aircraft looks attractive. The business is primarily focused on general aviation, so there is a cyclical demand risk, but we also note that the company focuses on the premium segment within the general aviation market and has pioneered safety solutions, while a growing installed base should help long-term growth in services revenues. I believe that the stock is attractively positioned for growth and rate the stock a strong buy.
